subcategory: South African Cape fynbos signature shrub

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; sugarbush protea requires excellent drainage — wet, heavy soils are fatal. Do not fertilize with phosphorus (phosphorus kills Proteaceae). Use only Proteaceae-specific or low-phosphorus fertilizers. It blooms in winter and spring, is adapted to fire, and produces large quantities of sweet nectar. Outstanding for birds (sunbirds in South Africa; hummingbirds in cultivation). The dried flower heads are widely used in the floral trade.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; sugarbush is the most widely distributed and historically significant protea species — the signature Cape fynbos plant of the mountains and coastal hills of the Western Cape from which the Cape Floral Kingdom takes much of its iconic visual identity. The flower heads are typical protea form — a rounded 'artichoke' of overlapping bracts enclosing the central mass of flowers — in white to cream-white to pale pink, sometimes streaked or flushed with deeper pink or rose, each individual flower head 4–5 inches across. They bloom in winter and spring (May–October in South Africa) and produce so much sweet nectar that the common name 'sugarbush' refers to the honey-like sweetness that children and sugarbirds alike consume directly from the flower — the traditional source of 'proteabosse stroop', a traditional Cape syrup. The nectar production and flower structure are extraordinary among ornamental plants; in cultivation, hummingbirds visit the flowers repeatedly. The large, dried flower heads, with their sculptural, papery bracts, are a major cut-flower and dried-flower export from South Africa. A magnificent signature plant for zone 9 Mediterranean-climate gardens — requiring the specific drainage, lean soil, and low-phosphorus conditions of its fynbos habitat.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; pincushion protea requires excellent drainage and absolutely no phosphorus fertilizer — phosphorus is lethal to all Proteaceae. Use only Proteaceae-specific or phosphorus-free fertilizers. It blooms in late winter through spring, producing large, spherical, brilliant-orange flower heads composed of densely packed, projecting styles (the 'pins' of the pincushion). Outstanding cut flower with long vase life. Does not tolerate frost below about 28°F/-2°C.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; pincushion protea is one of the most spectacular and widely grown of all South African fynbos plants in cultivation — producing in late winter through spring extraordinary, spherical to dome-shaped flower heads of brilliant orange to orange-red to golden-orange composed of densely packed, long, tubular perianths each tipped with a projecting style that creates the exact, unmistakable texture of a pincushion densely stuck with orange-headed pins, 4–5 inches across per head. The flowers are bold, vivid, warm orange — a color that dominates and energizes any planting composition — and have exceptional vase life as cut flowers (10–14 days), making the pincushion protea one of the important South African cut flower exports worldwide. In the garden it forms a substantial, mounding, evergreen shrub with attractive, leathery, blue-gray-green, heart-shaped leaves (giving the species name cordifolium) providing year-round structure. Like all Proteaceae it requires excellent drainage, lean soil, and absolutely no phosphorus fertilizer. The parent species of many important Leucospermum hybrids. A signature plant for California and zone 9 Gulf Coast Mediterranean-style gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; highveld protea is distinctly more cold-hardy than the Cape fynbos proteas — native to the summer-rainfall grasslands and rocky highveld of the Drakensberg and Mpumalanga at 4000–6000 feet elevation, where frosts are a regular feature. It tolerates harder frosts than P. repens or P. cynaroides (to zone 7b) and summer rainfall — making it more adaptable to non-Mediterranean climates. Like all Proteaceae, it requires excellent drainage and no phosphorus fertilizer.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; highveld protea is the most cold-tolerant widely available Protea species — the protea of the summer-rainfall highveld of South Africa, growing naturally at high altitude in the rocky grasslands and cliff faces of the Drakensberg, Mpumalanga, and Limpopo where hard frosts occur regularly in winter. This cold tolerance (to zone 7b) expands the Protea genus into gardens significantly colder than the Mediterranean-climate zones where most proteas are restricted. The flower heads are medium-sized, cup-shaped, white to creamy-white to pale pink, somewhat less flamboyant than the showier Cape species but still distinctively beautiful. It grows into a small tree with a characteristic, gnarled, fire-adapted trunk and gray, fire-resistant bark. Unlike the Cape fynbos proteas adapted to winter rainfall, P. caffra comes from a summer-rainfall climate — making it significantly more tolerant of summer humidity and rain, and suitable for a wider range of garden climates including the Gulf Coast and Southeast where summer dryness cannot be guaranteed. An important species for gardeners who want to grow true Protea in zones 7–9 outside the Mediterranean climate zone.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or well-drained soil in full sun; pincushion hakea requires excellent drainage and lean soil — it is native to dry, sandy soils of southwestern Australia. Do not fertilize with phosphorus. It blooms in autumn through winter (April–July in Australia; October–January in the Northern Hemisphere), providing winter color. The flowers are outstanding for nectar-feeding birds (honeyeaters in Australia; hummingbirds in cultivation).

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; pincushion hakea is one of the most spectacular Australian shrubs — a large, erect shrub or small tree from southwestern Australia producing in autumn and winter remarkable, spherical, sea-urchin-like flower heads 2–3 inches across: dense, globular clusters of cream-yellow to pale-cream tubular flowers from which project numerous long, curved, crimson to bright-red styles — the combined effect of the cream flower and scarlet projecting styles creating the distinctive 'pincushion' or 'sea urchin' appearance from which the common names are drawn. The contrast of the cream-colored flowers and brilliant red styles is vivid and immediately eye-catching. The leaves are long, narrow, lance-shaped, blue-green to gray-green, providing a fine-textured, somewhat willow-like foliage year-round. Blooming in autumn and winter when few other dramatic flowers are available, it provides an exceptional, long-lasting (the flowers persist on the shrub for weeks) winter display and, in cultivation, proves to be a reliable hummingbird attractor. Like all Proteaceae, it requires excellent drainage, lean soil, and absolutely no phosphorus-based fertilizer.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Bauer's grevillea is notable among grevilleas for being one of the few species that tolerates partial shade — most grevilleas are strictly full-sun plants. It forms a compact, spreading, ground-hugging mound and blooms most heavily in spring but flowers sporadically year-round. No phosphorus fertilizer — fatal to Proteaceae. Named for the Austrian botanical artist Franz Bauer.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Bauer's grevillea is a compact, spreading, relatively low-growing Australian grevillea — native to the rocky, somewhat shaded forest margins and valley floor habitats of New South Wales — that stands apart from many grevillea species in its greater shade tolerance, making it one of the few Proteaceae useful for planting in the dappled shade or semi-shade of woodland margins and north-facing hillsides. The flowers are in the typical grevillea 'toothbrush' or clustered style — dense clusters of small, curved, tubular red to pink-red flowers with projecting, hooked styles that create the characteristic grevillea flower cluster — produced in greatest abundance in spring but appearing sporadically through the year in favorable mild-winter climates. The foliage is small, dark green, and finely textured, creating a compact, spreading, mat-like mound that works well as a low-maintenance groundcover shrub in the right conditions. Like all Proteaceae, it requires lean soil and absolutely no phosphorus fertilizer. Named for Franz Bauer, the celebrated Austrian botanical artist at Kew whose extraordinary illustrations documented the new Australian flora in the early 19th century.
